However, gold was also long-term overbought many times during its bull market run in the 2000s, so I believe this is less important than the bullish trend indicators noted above.

This last chart shows the monthly gold price relative to the S&P 500 (top clip) since 1995. The message is similar to the absolute price chart shown above. Again, gold broke above the trend line that contained prices from 2011 to early 2024. Relative long-term monthly momentum is also positive (third clip), as is quarterly momentum (fourth clip). And on a relative basis, gold appears less "long-term overbought".

These technical indicators suggest the bull market trend of gold is likely to remain your friend for a while longer.

Conclusion

Based on our "four steps to bull and bear profits" analysis, the gold bull market looks like it has more room to run, particularly relative to stocks. This is based on its attractive valuation, favorable economic and fundamental factors, contrarian bullish sentiment indicators and bullish trend indicators.

The best asset class at any given time historically (at least prior to the advent of Bitcoin, which remains highly correlated to stocks) has been either stocks or gold. Based on my analysis, I believe gold is likely to outshine stocks as the best asset in the coming years.

data.title: Braskem: A New Shareholder Specializing In Turnarounds Could Be The Trigger
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying Braskem (NYSE:BAK) shares following the takeover bid by Brazilian businessman Nelson Tanure. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on June 14, 2024.

In this article, I intend to analyze the offer in detail. We will analyze the context of the proposal, understand the risks if it is not accepted, and link this to the company's latest results and valuation.

Corporate Profile

Braskem is a Brazilian multinational petrochemical company. The company has over 8,000 employees and stands out as a leader in the production of resins such as polyethylene, polypropylene and PVC. Below we have an overview of its operations and production capacity.

Another interesting point is to understand the company's corporate structure. As we can see below, Novonor (formerly Odebrecht) owns 38.3% of the company, while Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) owns 36.1% and minority shareholders own 25.5%. There is also a shareholders' agreement.

Purchase Offer

Investors were recently surprised by investor Nelson Tanure’s offer to buy Braskem. Braskem shares rose more than 9% on the day of the news. Below, I will provide a detailed analysis of the offer and who the investor is.

Who is Nelson Tanure?

Nelson Tanure is a Brazilian investor specializing in corporate restructuring. The investor has significant stakes in companies such as Gafisa (OTCPK:GFASY), Light (OTCPK:LGSXY), Prio (OTCPK:PTRRY), and now intends to become a key shareholder in Braskem.

The Purchase Proposal In Detail

Tanure’s offer is aimed at acquiring NSP Investimentos — a holding company controlled by Novonor, which indirectly controls Braskem. If the offer is accepted, it would give the investor indirect control of the company.

The offer is under due diligence and there are no further details, such as governance terms or price. However, the analysis of the offer should include analyses of compliance with obligations set forth in the shareholders' agreement, as well as negotiations with Braskem's creditor banks.

It is important to highlight that the sale could be strategic for Novonor (formerly Odebrecht), given that its judicial recovery includes $13 billion in debts subject to judicial recovery, and $14.5 billion in extrajudicial recoveries, with Braskem shares being used as collateral.

My Analysis On The Possible Agreement

Braskem's performance continues to be linked to petrochemical spreads, and there is no consensus on how a new shareholder would bring relevant improvements to operations and governance

However, investor Tanure enjoys a great reputation for carrying out large turnarounds, such as Prio SA (OTCPK:PTRRY), which became the largest private Brazilian oil company in the last 10 years.

The timing of the purchase could not be better, given that the company is at its lowest prices in history due to the weak fundamentals of the petrochemical sector, and the entry of an investor of this size could be a major trigger for an improvement in the company.

The biggest challenge to completing the deal is environmental liabilities, such as the sinking of an entire neighborhood in Maceió, the capital of the Brazilian state of Alagoas, due to a Braskem salt mine. These liabilities affected LyondellBasell's efforts to acquire Braskem in 2019.

Latest Earning Results

Despite the complex sectoral situation, the company reported good results in the 1st quarter of 2025. The company reported $113 million in net income, reversing a net loss of $273 million in the 1st quarter of 2024.

The company noted that polyethylene and chemical spreads were a positive highlight in the quarter. This was due to the lower supply in the American market and the volatility in raw material costs due to geopolitical uncertainties.

Valuation

Braskem and its competitors are exporters, so the financial result may distort the net profit. Thus, the most assertive way to analyze Braskem's valuation is to use the EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its competitors.

The average EV/EBITDA multiple of its peers is 9x, while Braskem's multiple is 7x, a 28% upside that supports my recommendation to buy the company. Although the acceptance of the offer could be the trigger for the upside to materialize, we must understand the risks of the thesis.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Petrobras is the main supplier of raw materials to Braskem and has significant influence over the company. Petrobras' position on the deal is still unknown, but it could be crucial to the conclusion of the deal.

The second risk concerns the tag along. If Novonor maintains a minority stake in the business and the investor Tanure merely becomes a party to the shareholder agreement, the tag along may not be triggered, and minority investors waiting for a liquidity event may be frustrated.

Another risk is that Braskem has already received numerous purchase offers, and none of them have had a positive conclusion. Petrochemical companies' margins are tight due to the downcycle, and Novonor may understand that this is not the best time to sell its stake.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's powerful tools recommend that investors hold on to their shares. This differs from my recommendation to buy the shares. The thesis is complex and investors should make a pragmatic analysis.

The Bottom Line

It is no news that the petrochemical sector is going through a less than encouraging cycle. Spreads are not very attractive, and companies' margins are getting tighter with each result. However, this is an excellent scenario for an investor who wants to pay a low price.

In my view, this is what Brazilian investor Nelson Tanure is seeing when he is interested in Braskem's assets. A deal could be a major trigger for a surge in prices due to the investor's history of turnarounds.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Braskem shares. In my view, the 28% upside is quite interesting and the risk/return ratio seems very attractive with the possible entry of such a relevant investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Debt Downgrade And Tariffs: The Economic Reset (Recession) Is Needed And It's Coming
data.description:
On May 16th, Moody's downgraded the U.S. credit rating due to a rising debt load. The stock market took this in stride (so far), although the bond market reacted by pushing yields a bit higher. It's easy to dismiss the downgrade, especially since downgrades in the past did not always prove to be consequential, but this could be the trigger for bigger events. I have been worried lately about the valuation of the stock market, along with the chance of a recession, and perhaps for something even worse that resets the value of many assets, including stocks, real estate and more. Although my base case is for at least a mild recession, I do think there is about a 15% chance of a very significant downturn coming, one that catches many people off guard, just like the 2008 Great Financial Crisis. Before the Great Financial Crisis of 2008 unfolded, there were small warning signs that were dismissed by some investors as being manageable or transitory, but later set off an avalanche. These concerns have led me to stay invested with some core holdings, but I have also raised my cash levels to around 80% of my portfolio, for now.

Why Conditions Appear Ripe For A Downturn

1) For many years now, we have seen that a "buy the dip" strategy works every time and I believe there is a whole generation of investors who have this ingrained in their mind. But, at some point, buying a 10 or 20% dip is not going to pay off, and it could just result in further losses. I believe this consistent buy-the-dip mentality has bred complacency which could lead to a bigger-than-average downturn for stocks someday. Complacency when it comes to the stock market means valuations might go higher than what is reasonable, but it might also mean a lot of investors are caught off guard and unprepared for a potential downturn, which in turn could make it more severe.

2) Valuations are at historically high levels as the S&P 500 Index (SPY) now trades for around 27 times earnings. Since 1950, the average price-to-earnings ratio has been about 20 times earnings, and this typically dips to around 16 times during recessions. If a market correction were to take stock valuations from about 27 down to 16, that would represent a significant decline. But even if the price-to-earnings ratio declines from around 27 to the historical average of about 20, that would represent a 25% pullback. A historically higher price-to-earnings ratio can make more sense when interest rates are extremely low as they were about 3 years ago, but those ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy) days are over and not likely coming back any time soon, because of the massive U.S. Government debt load.

3) There is a standoff going on now, whereby the interest payments on the U.S. Government are at unsustainable levels and rising, and yet the only way to get the interest payments back to a less concerning level might be to have the type of economic rest that a recession would provide. A recession would re-price risk assets much lower and investors would flock to the "safety" of bonds even if a 10-year Treasury Note was offering a yield of 2.25% in a recession, instead of roughly twice that level that it currently yields now. There could be new-found appreciation for a 2.25% yield, when you are watching risk assets like stocks plunge 25% or more. Significantly lower rates are what the U.S. Government needs, and a recession might be the only and inevitable solution.

4) Tariff headwinds are creating a new round of economic uncertainty and downward pressure on the economy. The stock market recouped most of the recent losses that it incurred after Liberation Day. This was due in part because Q1 earnings were generally solid, and also because President Trump seemed to ease on some of his tariff plans and suggested many trade deals are coming soon. However, the recent market rally could be a head fake as Q2 and Q3 earnings reports could start to show the real pain of tariffs. It's worth noting that some consumers resorted to stocking up on some items due to the fear that tariffs would make these items more expensive later, so this led to higher demand for many items from appliances to electronics like mobile phones. This pulls forward of demand should be over soon and set up the economy for weakness in the second half of 2025.

Not surprisingly, there are plenty of people and businesses who do not support the tariff policies of the Trump Administration, and these tariff policies are being challenged in court. On May 28th, a trade court ruled that President Trump had exceeded his authority with the Liberation Day tariffs. However, this respite was very short-lived because within hours thereafter, an appeals court ruled that the tariffs could stay in place for now. Even if the court ruling was, or is allowed to stand, President Trump has other tools and potential loopholes that he could use, including section 232 tariffs which could be implemented for "national security" purposes. Section 338 tariffs would allow the President to impose levies of up to 50% against countries that "discriminate" against the United States. Since national security and discrimination are potentially broad and subjective terms, this and other potential legal options could give President Trump everything he needs to continue imposing his tariff policies.

5) The labor market has been strong and unemployment is low, almost so low that it might have nowhere to go except higher, which could mean job losses are coming. Many companies are facing uncertainty now and also looking to become more efficient and this seems to be resulting in job cuts, even for healthy companies.

Here Are The Warning Signs

1) Gold is sending a big potential warning sign to stock market investors, as it continues to surge and hit new all-time highs. I was bullish on gold and in March 2024, I suggested gold mining stocks could rally 40% in this article; however, I was wrong and the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) has seen gains of about 70% since my article was published. Obviously, I am not as bullish on the gold sector now, and it could be due for a pullback, but I feel that it is increasingly alarming if gold continues to rise from current levels. If it does continue to rise, it seems to be a sign that investors are losing confidence in paper assets and possibly in the financial system in general. If uncertainty in the world and the economy calms down we could see a big pullback in gold, but there is also a chance that the recent gains in gold are just the tip of the iceberg and a warning sign that the rise in gold is happening because of a structural and negative change in our financial system and in the economy.

2) Bitcoin is considered by some to be digital gold, and it has also recently hit new all-time highs. This is another potential warning sign that investors are looking to park assets outside of the traditional financial system and be in an "asset" that is not (in theory) subject to money printing or government manipulation.

3) Transportation stocks are also sending a potential warning signal of a coming economic slowdown. Just take a look at FedEx (FDX) and UPS (UPS) shares, which have been in decline for a while. Investors are right to believe that some of the decline in these stocks is due to competitive threats from Amazon (AMZN) as it continues to increasingly deliver more small packages. However, even if you look at the shares of truckload shippers like JB Hunt (JBHT) which do not have the same competitive threats from Amazon, the downdraft in the transportation sector is a potentially major warning signal that a recession is looming.

4) Oil stocks are sending another potential warning sign of a looming recession. Supply is part of the equation when it comes to oil prices, but so is demand and the decline in oil prices is not signalling a robust global economy going forward.

5) Credit card and student loan delinquencies have been rising, and soft data such as consumer sentiment is at very low levels. Hard data such as a major deterioration in the labor market typically lags behind the soft data, so real signs of weakness in the hard data could be coming next.

6) President Trump just fired a new round of attacks in the trade war, with a suggestion that Apple (AAPL) could face tariffs of 25% on iPhones that are not made in the U.S.A. and that Europe could face 50% tariffs. Many investors are presuming that President Trump is bluffing, but even if tariff rates are much lower than 50%, the settled-upon rates are likely to be much higher than what they were previously. Furthermore, these latest threats represent an escalation in the trade war and Europe could respond by putting tariffs on services like software and also increasing regulations and fines on U.S. tech giants.

Potential escalations aside, reaching an acceptable trade deal with certain countries, especially important ones like China, might be harder to get than what the market currently expects. The latest updates on the negotiations with China are not positive as Treasury Secretary Bessent said on May 29th, that trade talks with China are "a bit stalled". This latest development shows that getting a trade deal with China and dozens of other countries might take much longer than expected and this could negatively impact the global economy.

7) There is a lot of new money in the stock market, and I believe valuations have been pushed up due to excessive speculation. There is also a lot of margin debt in the stock market, which is nearly $900 billion today. In a sharp downturn, this could force liquidation-type selling in the stock market, and it is worth noting that high levels of margin debt are usually what is seen at market tops, not market bottoms.

Here Are Some Highly Regarded People Who Seem To Be Getting Ready For A Recession Or Financial Crisis

1) Jamie Dimon, the CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), was well-prepared for the 2008 Great Financial Crisis and his bank bought up many failing banks. Mr. Dimon has been raising concerns about the complacency he sees in the stock market, as well as concerns about the economy. Some investors may dismiss his views since the economy has remained strong, but major economic changes can take time to play out, and I believe he will be proven right once again.

2) Ray Dalio, the billionaire founder of Bridgewater Associates, has also been warning about U.S. debt levels and the global economy. He is concerned about an economic downturn that could be worse than a recession.

3) Warren Buffett has been selling positions in stocks like Apple and Bank of America (BAC), and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B) now has about $347 billion in cash or equivalents on the balance sheet. I take this as a strong sell signal and suggestion to raise cash levels.

Here Is How The Economy And Stock Market Could Spiral Downward

With rates already at levels we have not seen in years, and with the threat of rates going higher, this presents more problems for the economy and the stock market. One way to lower high interest rates is to slow the economy. The Trump Administration must be aware of this option, although it is risky with the midterm elections. Higher interest rates will eventually lead to a recession. Tariffs could exacerbate the situation and also create enough economic pain and a significant downturn that leads to the lower interest rates the U.S. Government needs in order to have a more manageable debt load. Here is a step-by-step summary of how high rates and tariffs could play out in the coming months:

1) Interest rates/bond yields remain high and possibly go higher as tariffs lead to inflation on some items.

2) These higher interest rates put pressure on the real estate market and as home prices decline, a negative wealth effect impacts consumer spending. Some homes bought during the peak of the Covid real estate boom with overbids in many cases, could see down payment equity evaporate and end up in bank foreclosure.

3) Banks could see foreclosures and other consumer and business loans go delinquent, creating a new wave of credit losses. However, the potentially even bigger problem for the banking industry is the huge losses on their bond portfolios, which are once again increasing as yields rise. Total unrealized losses for the banks just surged by $118.4 billion (a 32% increase) from the prior quarter. As shown below, unrealized losses for the banks now total a whopping $482.5 billion and this could be poised to rise significantly if yields rise.

4) If banks are suddenly faced with credit losses from consumer and business loans, as well as real estate foreclosures, and then the added losses from their bond portfolios, this could lead to more banks becoming insolvent. And this could lead to another financial crisis that resets asset prices back down to historical averages including real estate and stock market price-to-earnings multiples.

With all this in mind, I believe some type of economic reset (recession) is inevitable. It might be the only solution for the U.S. Government, as it tries to make the debt load payments more manageable through the much lower interest rates that typically occur in the wake of a major recession.

How I Could Be Wrong

AI could lead to a new boom in the economy and the stock market if it creates the productivity gains that many analysts are expecting. The U.S. economy might see growth increase late this year and into 2026 if President Trump succeeds in extending tax cuts. President Trump's plans to focus on deregulation could also lead to higher corporate profits, which could lift stock valuations.

In Summary

There are many red flags that investors should be heeding right now, but many are not, and this might continue until we get the hard data that confirms a recession. The problem is that if you wait until the hard data confirms a recession, it will be too late to prepare for it, as equity prices will move down quickly. I am hoping that Ray Dalio's concerns about something worse than a recession do not occur, and I am bullish in the long run, but for now, I believe it is time to prepare for a potentially perfect storm and I believe it makes sense to focus on preserving capital at this time. When you can get a yield of roughly 5% in a money market account, the stock market does not appear to offer an attractive risk/reward ratio. In addition, cash parked in money market accounts could be positioned to take advantage of much lower prices with a stock market correction that typically occurs during recessions. Please let me know what you think in the comments below!

data.title: What Canada's Election Results Mean For Nuclear Energy
data.description:
The liberals won in Canada again last month. With their fourth consecutive win, Mark Carney, who replaced Justin Trudeau as the Prime Minister as the leader of the party in March, is now at the helm for the next four years.

Going by Carney's earlier ideas, this win is expected to be a positive for the nuclear energy sector since Canada plays a pivotal role in the supply chain with its uranium reserves, which are the biggest in the world after Australia and Kazakhstan (see chart below).

Carney's support of nuclear energy

Carney, who has earlier served as the governor of the Bank of England, has spoken well of the sector in the past, calling it a "great opportunity". Specifically, he has mentioned Canada's uranium reserves as an advantage. He has also mentioned "major nuclear companies" that give Canada an edge in this regard, like reactor developer Westinghouse as well as the big uranium miner Cameco Corporation (CCJ).

The Westinghouse acquisition

His mention of these companies deserves a background. Westinghouse was acquired jointly by Cameco and Brookfield Asset Management in 2023. While Brookfield has a 51% share in the company, Cameco owns the remainder.

Interestingly, the acquisition was made with Brookfield Global Transition Fund during the time that Carney was its co-head (from 2020 until early 2025), before he threw his hat into the political ring. Essentially, there couldn't be a bigger endorsement of the sector than this.

No net zero without nuclear energy

Except, as it turns out, there is. In September last year, Carney tweeted, "There’s no net zero without nuclear". This was in relation to big financial institutions' support to nations signing the Declaration to Triple Nuclear Energy by 2050 at the COP28. These institutions included the likes of Citi (C), Goldman Sachs (GS), Morgan Stanley (MS), Bank of America (BAC) and of course Brookfield (BAM).

The Liberal energy policy

The key question now is, what Carney's supportive position on nuclear energy means for the sector going forward. In speaking of the country's energy policy under his leadership, he says, "Canada has a tremendous opportunity to be the world’s leading energy superpower, in both clean and conventional energy...".

For nuclear energy, this reflects in the government's intent to encourage investment in critical minerals, of which there are 34 on Canada's list, including uranium and lithium (see chart below). The Liberal government aims to attract investments into exploration projects as well as support the funding of projects, besides expanding the reach of the Critical Mineral Exploration Tax Credit.

These initiatives may well speed up the progress of uranium mining in the country, which has a number of mines in various stages of development right now. As a big miner on the world stage, Cameco is arguably the most developed Canadian miner right now. But on the other hand, there are those like Denison Mines (DNN), which are some time away from operationalizing, but hold much promise going by their resources in the uranium-rich Athabasca Basin (check) and the net present valuations available.

Uranium stocks show muted response...

However, despite the promise, uranium stocks didn't see big gains immediately following Carney's win. The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM), for example, was up after the new, but only by 2%. To put this gain in context, at that time, it was the sixteenth biggest single-day gain year-to-date [YTD]. Similarly, Cameco also responded well to the development but was also up by just 3%. It has seen bigger single-day increases eight times in 2025 alone so far.

...due to challenges in trading with the US

There was a good reason for the stocks' muted response. And that's the recent Canadian relationship with the US. Carney has made no bones about his displeasure with the Trump administration's recent conduct.

Now, this is problematic in any case, but particularly so for nuclear energy. Even though Canada gets a substantial 15% of its electricity from the source, some 85% of its uranium is exported, a significant proportion of which goes to the US. While the US government has been soft on uranium, with just a 10% tariff on it, the challenges between the US and Canada go beyond just tariffs. In the month since Carney has been at the helm, there has been no improvement in the relations between the two countries, on the contrary, the Trump administration's offer to Canada to become the 51st US state has predictably caused an upset.

This then suggests that there could be uncertainty, at least in the near term, regarding uranium exports to the US, which is the biggest consumer of the commodity in the world.

What can go right

But going by the US's focus on nuclear energy, it's still possible that things can get ironed out, at least on this front. In the words of the US Energy Secretary, Chris Wright, "American Nuclear Renaissance" is called for and Trump's executive orders on the sector from last week only back this up.

To achieve robust growth in nuclear energy, it's clear that the US needs to maintain healthy relations with its trading partners, considering that it imports almost all uranium. And the biggest chunk of 27% of these imports come from Canada.

There can also be a rebalancing of trading relations over time for Canada if its equation with the US worsens, impacting trade. While there's no denying the significance of the US in uranium consumption, the two next biggest nuclear energy suppliers – China and France – are together fairly close to it. And with 151 reactors under development around the world— ex. US, even if Canada's supply to the US were to wane, there might still be plenty of takers.

Uranium sees an uptick

In any case, the uptick in uranium stocks has started once again after they slumped earlier in the year. This is in no small measure due to the Trump executive orders last week. The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM) saw an over 12% jump on the news. This is important considering that over 30% of its assets in just the top 10 holdings are in Canadian uranium miners like Cameco, NexGen Energy (NXE), and Denison Mines.

Over time, as more clarity emerges on the Canadian energy policy as well as the interactions between Canada and the US, there are more likely to be gains than not. In sum, the outlook for the sector continues to be positive, even though the present geopolitical stresses are clouding it.
